stories.headline: Colorado lawmakers introduce bipartisan bill to restrict sports betting practices, protect minors
stories.summary:
A bipartisan coalition of Colorado legislators has introduced Senate Bill 131, which would impose new restrictions on sports betting operators in the state. The bill is sponsored by Rep. Steven Woodrow and Rep. Dan Woog in the House, and Sens. Matt Ball and Rod Pelton in the Senate. It has been assigned to the House Finance Committee.

The legislation would limit customers to five deposits within a 24-hour period, ban the use of credit cards for sports betting, and prohibit proposition bets — wagers placed on specific in-game events such as passing yards or the first team to score. The bill would also restrict push notifications and text messages from betting operators to Colorado account holders and place guardrails on the language permitted in sports betting advertisements.

Coloradans wagered more than $6 billion in online sports bets in 2025, a 130% increase from 2020, according to the Colorado Department of Revenue. Supporters of the bill point to research showing that 36% of boys aged 11 to 17 reported gambling in the past year as evidence of the need for tighter regulation.

Colorado voters legalized sports betting through Proposition DD in 2019, with the majority of revenue directed to the Colorado Water Plan Grant Program. SB 131 would also require betting operators to submit annual transactional data to the state Gaming Division, which would publish a report on that data every three years beginning in 2029.

stories.headline: Grass fire burns 10 acres in Thornton, injures five and prompts evacuations near I-25
stories.summary:
A grass fire broke out Wednesday morning near Pinnacle Charter High School at 8412 Huron St. in Thornton, burning approximately 10 acres of dry fields and charring vehicles before firefighters achieved full containment at 2:07 p.m. More than 100 firefighters from across the Denver metro area responded to the blaze, which started at 11:30 a.m. amid powerful, gusty winds that also kept much of the Front Range under a red flag warning.

Four firefighters and one civilian were injured in the fire, though Thornton Fire Chief Stephen Kelley said none of the injuries appeared life-threatening. Smoke from the fire reduced visibility on Interstate 25 to the point that state transportation officials closed the highway in both directions for more than an hour. The fire also caused more than 3,000 Xcel Energy customers to lose power, with most outages restored by Wednesday evening.

Police officers went door to door to carry out evacuations, and city officials issued alerts through the statewide Integrated Public Alert and Warning System. Kelley noted that Thornton is currently in the process of switching to a new city-level emergency alert system and does not have one in place. Pinnacle Charter High School and several nearby businesses were also evacuated, with the evacuation order lifted at 3:30 p.m.

No homes were destroyed in the fire, which ignited on a greenbelt between a residential neighborhood and commercial properties. While nearby business buildings did not appear to be structurally damaged, rows of cars in adjacent parking lots were burned. The cause of the fire remains under investigation, and firefighters were expected to remain on scene overnight to monitor hot spots.

stories.headline: Denver police arrest 20-year-old man on first-degree murder charge in Valentine's Day house party shooting
stories.summary:
Denver police announced the arrest of Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera, 20, in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred during a house party in the early morning hours of Feb. 14 in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue. Yriarte-Valera faces a first-degree murder charge after he allegedly shot and killed a man who was found suffering from multiple gunshot wounds on the building's front porch. First responders carried the victim into the street to render aid, but he was pronounced dead at the scene.

A second victim, an adult woman, was transported to a hospital with a gunshot wound to her ankle. Residents in the area had reported a loud party and multiple street fights approximately 30 minutes before the shooting occurred. Officers conducting a sweep of the residence found large amounts of suspected alcohol containers, speakers, and a light display in the basement, as well as 25 spent 9mm cartridge casings in the street and 10 more inside the home and on the staircase.

Investigators interviewed multiple Spanish-speaking witnesses with the assistance of a bilingual officer. Witnesses consistently reported observing a fight involving multiple men and one woman in the street, during which a man fired multiple gunshots. According to a probable cause affidavit, the woman shot in the ankle said the altercation was connected to a conflict involving her ex-boyfriend, with whom there had been documented and undocumented domestic violence incidents.

The affidavit, which was heavily redacted, indicated that the woman's current boyfriend also fired a handgun two to three times into the air during the confrontation. Investigators used witness accounts and other evidence gathered at the scene to identify Yriarte-Valera as the individual who shot the deceased victim.

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston signs executive order directing police to intervene if ICE agents use excessive force
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston signed an executive order Thursday directing Denver Police Department officers to intervene and, if necessary, detain U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents observed using excessive force against protesters. The order also bans federal agents from using city-owned property — including overnight shelters, community centers, libraries, and facilities such as the Denver Coliseum — for immigration enforcement operations.

Johnston announced the order while standing in front of the City and County Building in downtown Denver, flanked by Police Chief Ron Thomas and other city leaders. He said the goal was to protect residents in the event of a large-scale ICE operation in the city, not to provoke President Donald Trump. City Attorney Miko Brown said the order is constitutional and described it as "responsible governance" rather than an act of defiance.

The executive order is intended to work in tandem with an ordinance the Denver City Council is expected to pass next week. That ordinance would prohibit all law enforcement officers — including federal agents — from covering their faces during detentions and arrests. Johnston acknowledged he has no specific information indicating that ICE or the Trump administration plans to send a surge of agents to Denver in the near term.

Denver, Aurora, and Colorado more broadly have been a recurring focus for the Trump administration during his second term, with the president repeatedly threatening the cities with increased immigration enforcement and the withdrawal of federal funds. Federal agencies have conducted ongoing immigration operations in the Denver area, though not at the scale seen recently in cities such as Chicago and Minneapolis. The mayor's order drew immediate national media attention.

stories.headline: Colorado's congressional delegation splits along party lines in response to Trump's State of the Union address
stories.summary:
Colorado's congressional delegation reacted sharply along partisan lines to President Donald Trump's State of the Union address Tuesday, with the state's four Republican House members praising the speech and its six Democratic members denouncing it. The address, which ran nearly two hours, covered topics including border security, energy production, tariffs, and what Trump described as the beginning of a national "Golden Age."

Republican members including Reps. Gabe Evans, Jeff Crank, Lauren Boebert, and Jeff Hurd issued statements and social media posts applauding the administration's record on border security, energy, and fiscal policy. Hurd, who lost Trump's endorsement over differences on tariff policy, described the speech as a chance to "reflect on that progress and focus on the work still ahead."

Democrats on the delegation offered pointed criticism. Sen. John Hickenlooper said ahead of the address that Trump would "tell us not to believe what we can see with our own eyes," citing unaffordable living costs and what he described as "lawless" ICE operations. Rep. Jason Crow of Aurora, who was among lawmakers the Department of Justice unsuccessfully sought to indict after he made a video telling troops they need not follow illegal orders, attended the speech alongside other lawmakers who appeared in that video.

Rep. Diana DeGette of Denver was the only delegation member who did not attend the address, stating on social media that she chose not to "legitimize" what she described as egregious actions by the administration. The split reactions reflected broader national divisions over Trump's second-term agenda and his characterization of the country's direction.

stories.headline: Douglas County wildfire burns more than 1,000 acres south of Franktown, prompts evacuations
stories.summary:
A wildfire scorched more than 1,000 acres of dry, grassy terrain south of Franktown in Douglas County on Tuesday, forcing the evacuation of nearby homes and a local elementary school. The Dahlberg fire was first reported at 12:39 p.m. near Dahlberg and Lake Gulch roads, an area roughly 8 miles south of Franktown and 8 miles east of Larkspur. Tinder-dry fuels, drought conditions, and winds up to 20 mph enabled the fire to spread rapidly, according to Douglas County Emergency Management Director Mike Alexander.

Sheriff's deputies contacted 20 homes threatened by the fire, and 17 of those households evacuated. Cherry Valley Elementary School initially sheltered students in place before releasing them early so parents and guardians could retrieve them; the school was fully evacuated by 4 p.m. Firefighters from across metro Denver, along with an air tanker from Colorado Springs and a helicopter from Broomfield, responded to the scene.

Firefighters achieved full containment on the 1,081-acre fire just before 5 p.m. No buildings or property were reported damaged, and the cause of the fire remains under investigation. Dahlberg Road was closed Tuesday night to support ongoing firefighting operations.

The fire came as Denver International Airport was also grappling with the effects of high winds, with the FAA ordering a ground stop and airlines delaying 385 flights and canceling 25 as of 6 p.m. Forecasters warned that gusty winds were expected to return Wednesday, with sustained speeds up to 35 mph and gusts up to 50 mph possible across the Front Range and Eastern Plains.

stories.headline: Denver Downtown Development Authority brings in Urban Land Institute to chart future of Denver Pavilions mall
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority has contracted with the Urban Land Institute to produce a recommendations report on the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and its adjacent parking lots. A ULI advisory services panel is scheduled to visit Denver from April 13 to 17, with a final report expected approximately one month later. The city is paying ULI $100,000 for the engagement.

The DDDA purchased the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. in December from Denver-based Gart Properties, which had defaulted on the property's loan, for $37 million. The authority also paid $23 million for two parking lots behind the mall along 15th Street from New York-based Brookfield Properties. The mall is approximately 60% leased and generates $5.3 million annually in rent, while the underground parking and adjacent lots bring in an additional $3.2 million per year.

Ahead of the ULI visit, the DDDA has issued two requests for proposals due March 13 — one seeking retail, dining, and entertainment leasing consultants to develop a repositioning vision, and a second seeking architecture and urban design firms to reimagine the property as "the catalytic centerpiece of a revitalized Upper Downtown." Gart Properties continues to manage the mall under a contract paying $379,000, which covers on-site maintenance, operations staff, and accounting services.

Denver Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the authority's preference is to transfer the property to private-sector ownership as quickly as possible rather than sign major new leases itself. The mall currently lacks a dedicated leasing team, though Gart can show spaces upon request. Two escalators along the 16th Street Mall frontage are among the property's known maintenance issues.

stories.headline: Upcoming Denver events: Dairy Block Petite Parade, Purim Drag Queen Bingo, 303 Day and more
stories.summary:
Several notable events are scheduled in the Denver metro area over the coming days. The eighth annual Dairy Block Petite Parade, a Mardi Gras-inspired event featuring miniature floats built on a shoebox base, takes place Sunday, March 1, from noon to 2 p.m. in the heated alley of the Dairy Block at 1800 Wazee St. in Lower Downtown. The free event is open to the public, with prizes awarded for categories including Most Original Float and Best Team Theme.

On Saturday, Feb. 28, the Mizel Arts and Culture Center presents the sixth annual Purim Drag Queen Bingo at the Staenberg-Loup Jewish Community Center Social Hall, 250 S. Dahlia St. The Garden of Eden-themed event runs from 7:30 to 10:30 p.m. and includes a costume contest, cash bar, and vegetarian appetizers. Tickets range from $50 to $75 and the event is restricted to ages 21 and older.

Tuesday, March 3 marks Buy Colorado Day — formerly 303 Day and now an official state holiday as of 2025 — with participating bars, restaurants, and boutiques across the Denver area offering themed products and special pricing. Indie 102.3 FM is hosting a free all-ages concert from 5 to 9 p.m. at Number Thirty Eight, featuring local artists Bruha, Graveyard Choir, and Cheap Perfume.

Also on Sunday, March 1, the running group Latinos Run Denver is hosting a free all-abilities 5K beginning at 8:30 a.m. outside REI Denver at 1416 Platte St. The event benefits the Ray of Hope Cancer Foundation and is followed by a brunch and screening of the documentary "The Life We Have" at The Outpost at 1635 Platte St., with a panel discussion featuring local advocates and patient resources.

stories.headline: Measles case linked to Denver International Airport and Littleton church prompts free vaccination clinics
stories.summary:
Colorado public health officials confirmed that a person with a measles infection traveled through Denver International Airport and attended a church service in Littleton while infectious. The traveler arrived Saturday on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami, landing at gate A83 at 9:15 a.m. Anyone present in that area of the airport between 9:15 a.m. and noon may have been exposed to the disease.

The traveler then attended a service and reception at St. Mary's Catholic Church, 6853 S. Prince St. in Littleton, between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday before returning to DIA that evening and departing on Frontier flight 1280 to Miami from gate A76 at 11:29 p.m. Individuals at the airport between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday may also have been exposed. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment said passengers on both flights would be notified directly by their state or local public health agencies.

Public health officials noted that because the traveler is fully vaccinated, the risk of transmission is lower than it would otherwise be, though transmission remains possible. Free measles vaccination clinics were established Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department in Englewood and at St. Mary's Catholic Church. Officials noted that receiving a vaccine within 72 hours of exposure may prevent infection or reduce its severity.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the highest single-year total since at least 1996, with most cases occurring among unvaccinated individuals. One measles case has been reported in Colorado so far in 2026, in Arapahoe County, according to the state health department's measles tracking dashboard.

stories.headline: Broncos set to hire Colorado Buffaloes defensive coordinator Robert Livingston to fill out defensive staff
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are set to hire Robert Livingston, the defensive coordinator at the University of Colorado, as their defensive passing-game coordinator, according to multiple sources with knowledge of the situation. The hire fills the vacancy created when Jim Leonhard departed in late January to become the Buffalo Bills' defensive coordinator. Livingston, 40, spent two seasons at Colorado after being hired by head coach Deion Sanders in February 2024.

During his first season in Boulder, Livingston oversaw a significant improvement in the Buffaloes' defense, which climbed from 124th in the FBS in 2023 to 42nd in 2024. Colorado's defense struggled in a turbulent 2025 season, however, recording just six interceptions across 12 games. Livingston's background includes more than a decade with the Cincinnati Bengals, where he began as a scout in 2012 before rising to coach the team's safeties and defensive backs from 2016 through the 2023 season.

Livingston has an established relationship with Broncos defensive coordinator Vance Joseph, who was the Bengals' defensive backs coach in 2014 while Livingston was on the scouting staff. Denver has also added former USC secondary coach Doug Belk in a defensive backs role, replacing both Leonhard and fired cornerbacks coach Addison Lynch, who has since joined the New York Giants.

Head coach Sean Payton told reporters at the NFL Combine on Tuesday that the team was "close" to finalizing its staff and expected to make official announcements by the end of the week. The Broncos finished tied for 18th in the NFL with 10 interceptions in the previous season, and Payton has emphasized generating more takeaways as a priority heading into 2026.
